There is no villain here. Mia and Sebastian do not fail each other through betrayal or cruelty, they are simply shaped by the quiet demands of adulthood. Their love is genuine, but love alone is not enough.La La Land isn’t a tragedy or a fairy tale. It’s a reflection on timing, sacrifice, and the lives we build alongside the love we outgrow. You leave the film heartbroken, but contemplative, aware that somewhere, in another version of your life, a different song is still playing.

100-word review: A colourful, dreamy throwback to old-school Hollywood musicals that’s easy to get swept up in, at least some of the time. Sometimes you can’t have both love and ambition, and chasing your dream might mean losing something else along the way — it's a message with staying power. The jazz storyline, while central, comes off as preachy however, with Gosling’s character reduced to a stubborn caricature; we get it Damien Chazelle, you like jazz... The song-and-dance sequences vary from good to serviceable, but don't really wow me overall. Then again, I'm just not much of a musical enthusiast to begin with.

La La Land is a visually stunning and emotionally resonant musical that blends old Hollywood charm with a modern love story. Directed by Damien Chazelle, the film follows aspiring actress Mia (Emma Stone) and jazz musician Sebastian (Ryan Gosling) as they chase their dreams in Los Angeles. The chemistry between the leads is electric, the soundtrack is unforgettable, and the cinematography is pure magic. More than just a romance, it’s a bittersweet meditation on ambition, sacrifice, and the paths we choose. A must-watch for dreamers.
